name: REDD+ Country Profiles Indicators
short_description: The purpose of the country profile is to provide an overview of the contextual conditions that affect the REDD+ policy environment in a specific country, along with a preliminary 3E (effective, efficient and equitable) assessment of the REDD+ proposals for that country. The profile is compiled based on reviews of existing literature, national and international data and reviews of legislation and policies, as well as selected expert interviews. As such, it provides the background and the preliminary analysis of the context in which national REDD+ strategies are being developed. To help structure the country profile and aid the assessment of the 3E as well as to allow comparison among countries and periods from period to period, each section is linked to a set of indicators. Indicators cover the basic description, forest governance and its issues, decentralization and links to deforestation and forest degradation and levels of equity as related to indigenous rights to carbon, land and trees. In final two sets help describe the political economy and REDD policy environment covering actors, policy events, incentives and governance.
primary_use_case: REDD+ policy assessment and comparative governance analysis
justification: Supports comparative REDD+ policy analysis, governance assessment, and climate policy planning

name: Mitigating risks of disease transmission in the wild meat food chain from forest to fork in Cameroon
short_description: This dataset contains the data underlying the project “Mitigating risks of disease transmission in the wild meat food chain from forest to fork in Cameroon.” Data were collected between October and November 2022 using structured surveys administered via Kobo Collect. The dataset comprises two components: (1) a survey of hunters and food preparers, and (2) a survey of vendors operating in three markets. Together, these data capture attitudes and practices related to wild meat handling across the food chain, including hunting, processing, preparation, sale, and consumption behaviors, as well as relevant contextual and demographic information where applicable. The data have been cleaned and reviewed for consistency, and any identifying information has been removed to ensure respondent confidentiality. This dataset can be used to replicate study findings and to support further research on zoonotic disease risk and food safety practices in wild meat value chains.

name: Wet chemistry data for a subset of AfSIS: Phase I archived soil samples
nominator:
Winowiecki, Leigh Ann (ICRAF)
McGrath, Steve P (Rothamsted Research)
short_description: This dataset contains a subset of the samples collected during the AfSIS Phase I project and was a collaborative effort between World Agroforestry (ICRAF) and Rothamsted Research. The soil samples were retrieved from ICRAF Soil Archive: https://worldagroforestry.org/output/icraf-soil-archive-physical-archive-systematically-collected-soil-samples and subject to wet chemical analysis at Rothamsted Research in the UK under a Global Challenges Research Fund project, "BBS/OS/GC/000014B: Chemical and Biological Assessment of AfSIS soils" funded through the UK Biotechnology and Biological Sciences Research Council. This dataset includes the Site, Cluster, Plot as well as the GPS coordinates and wet chemistry data from 2002 samples collected from 18 countries and 51 LDSF sites. The original data collection was part of the AfSIS Phase I project, funded by the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation (BMGF) and took place between 2009-2013. ICRAF and CIAT contributed the Site, Cluster, Plot and GPS coordinates for the soil samples, ICRAF organized the sub-sampling of the soil samples from the ICRAF physical archive in Nairobi and Rothamsted analysed the soil samples in the UK in 2017 and 2018. Visit our websites here: https://worldagroforestry.org/landhealth and https://www.rothamsted.ac.uk/. The AfSIS Phase I project funded by the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation (BMGF) from 2009-2013, aimed to provide a consistent baseline of soil information across sub-Saharan Africa (SSA). Led by CIAT-TSBF, partners included: ISRIC, CIESIN, The Earth Institute at Columbia University and World Agroforestry (ICRAF). ICRAF led the systematic assessments of soil health using the Land Degradation Surveillance Framework (LDSF), which was developed at ICRAF, http://landscapeportal.org/blog/2015/03/25/the-land-degradation-surveillance-framework-ldsf/. LDSF sites were randomized using spatial stratification based on Koeppen-Geiger Climate zones across 19 countries in SSA. In total 60 LDSF sites were sampled. Soil samples were collected using the LDSF at two depths, 0-20 cm (labelled Topsoil) and 20-50 cm (labelled Subsoil). In each LDSF site, approximately 320 standard soil samples were collected. All of these were also scanned using MIR Spectroscopy and are available on Dataverse here: Vågen, Tor-Gunnar;Winowiecki, Leigh Ann;Desta, Luseged;Tondoh, Ebagnerin Jérôme;Weullow, Elvis;Shepherd, Keith;Sila, Andrew, 2020, "Mid-Infrared Spectra (MIRS) from ICRAF Soil and Plant Spectroscopy Laboratory: Africa Soil Information Service (AfSIS) Phase I 2009-2013", https://doi.org/10.34725/DVN/QXCWP1, World Agroforestry - Research Data Repository, V1.
primary_use_case: Soil health assessment, land degradation analysis, modelling input
justification: Foundational African soil chemistry and spectroscopy dataset with broad reuse potential across land health and restoration analytics.
output_variable_type: Soil organic carbon, total nitrogen, soil chemistry variables, MIR spectral properties
cgiar_programs: CGIAR Research Program on Water, Land and Ecosystems

name: Evaluating the restoration of the commons: LDSF field data
short_description: The dataset is based on ecological field data collection carried out in prioritized common land areas in 288 treated and matched control villages in the states of Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Odisha, and Rajasthan. It is based on the Land Degradation Survelliance Framework (LDSF) methodology. The LDSF methodology typically follows a standardized sampling design, where clusters are selected within 10-by-10-kilometer sentinel sites and then 10 0.1 hectare plots are randomly selected within each cluster. Each plot is then divided into four 0.01-hectare sub-plots from which field measurements are subsequently taken. We adapted this sampling approach to meet the unique needs of our study. Specifically, we treated each prioritized common land area as a sampling cluster. We then randomly sampled 10 plots (including their associated four sub-plots) within each. We then took inventories of all the trees, shrubs, and saplings in each of the 40 sampled sub-plots. We further randomly selected two of the plots for water infiltration measurement, which took place in the central sub-plot of the sampled plots in question. Given the time and effort involved, it was not possible to take these measurements in all 10 plots.
justification: Strong adaptation and restoration relevance with reusable LDSF methodology and operational restoration monitoring applicability
output_variable_type: Vegetation measurements, infiltration rates, restoration indicators, tree/shrub inventories
cgiar_programs: CGIAR Research Program on Water, Land and Ecosystems
contact_email_source: IWMI/WLE CGIAR profile; Google Scholar verified cgiar.org

name: Biophysical baseline assessment within the KCEP-CRAL action areas in Kenya, using the LDSF
short_description: These data were collected within the Kenya Cereal Enhancement Programme-Climate Resilient Agricultural Livelihoods (KCEP-CRAL) Window. This study was conducted within the action sites of the Kenya Cereal Enhancement Programme-Climate Resilient Agricultural Livelihoods (KCEP-CRAL) Window, which is a programme supported by the Government of Kenya and International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD) within the Adaptation for Smallholder Agriculture Programme (ASAP). The project assessed key indicators of land and soil health in order to understand drivers of degradation, and monitor changes over time using the Land Degradation Surveillance Framework (LDSF) methodology (http://landscapeportal.org/blog/2015/03/25/the-land-degradation-surveillance-framework-ldsf/). The LDSF provides a field protocol for measuring indicators of the "health" of an ecosystem. The LDSF was developed by the World Agroforestry (ICRAF) in response to the need for consistent field methods and indicator frameworks to assess land health in landscapes. The framework has been applied in projects across the global tropics, and is currently one of the largest land health databases globally with more than 30,000 observations, shared at http://landscapeportal.org. This project will benefit from existing data in the LDSF database, while at the same time contributing to these critically important global datasets through on-going data collection. Earth Observation (EO) data will be combined with the LDSF framework to develop the outputs for the project, including land degradation and soil health. Specific Activities on the ICRAF Component as Stated in the Agreement 1. Develop survey methodology detailing study design, methodology, tools, work plan and timelines documented 2. Procure LDSF field survey equipment 3. Conduct five LDSF surveys across the KCEP-CRAL action areas 4. Process, analyse and document the soil samples 5. Conduct Earth Observation-based assessment of biophysical indicators over time 6. Conduct capacity development opportunities with members of the PCU M&E staff and Government counterparts on LDSF field methodology This dataset contains LDSF field data from five LDSF sites in Kenya totalling 787 LDSF plots . It also contains the predictions using MIR Spectroscopy for 1500 Topsoil (0-20cm) and Subsoil (20-50cm) samples for six soil variables, including soil organic carbon, total nitrogen and pH.

name: Biophysical Soil and Land Health Assessment in the Gambia
short_description: The Land Degradation Surveillance Framework (LDSF) survey was a collaborative effort among a number of national universities, government technical services, national soil and forestry departments, research institutions and soil laboratories. Field teams spent about five weeks in the field observing vegetation conditions, land use, and visible erosion and taking soil samples. Soil samples were analyzed for a suite of physical and chemical properties. Analyses of these data were designed to provide an understanding of the landscape status based on easily discernible characteristics, particularly by site. The analysis also provides spatially explicit analysis of these findings as digital maps that can be used to provide very site-specific information on the health of soil and vegetation throughout the three LDSF sites. The aim was to provide some generalizable key problems and straightforward management recommendations. The field surveys took place in February 2021, with three field team working simultaneously. These surveys were led by the ICRAF team in Gambia in collaboration with the soil and forestry departments. The LDSF field training and data collection was led by ICRAF national team in Gambia, while the soil and forest departments of Gambia provided logistics support during the field work and data collection. This activity was conducted by World Agroforestry (ICRAF) as an output of the project, ‘Large-Scale Ecosystem-Based Adaptation in The Gambia River Basin: Developing a Climate Resilient, Natural Resource-Based Economy’. The project is funded by the Green Climate Fund and implemented by the Government of The Gambia with the support of United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP). In total, 474 plots of 1000 m2 were sampled: 160 plots in Changai, 158 plots in Dumboto and 156 plots in Gambisarra
primary_use_case: Land health assessment and climate resilience planning
justification: Contemporary land health dataset supporting resilience and restoration analysis within national contexts.

name: Dataset for supporting the net agronomic assessment of yield limiting factors in maize production in Machakos county, Kenya
short_description: This dataset is used for a holistic analysis of the costs, benefits, and risks of on-farm soil and plant health management. The dataset was produced in 2017 by a combination of field measurements and farmer surveys. It was collected for a research study aimed at identifying and testing accurate, consistent, and cost-effective measurement tools and methodologies for evaluating the outcomes of agricultural projects. Soils data was analysed by wet spectral methods to generate estimates of the Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (K), and Potassium (P) levels in the soils which was then used as inputs for a stochastic crop production model. The decision model consisted of two main sections targeting interactions between biotic factors (rainfall variability, availability of soil nutrients, risk of drought and temperature) and abiotic factors (farm management practices/intensity of farm management). With the two datasets, we ran a risk-return model to project the productivity of maize production and highlight yield-limiting factors. The project was funded by Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation and TechnoServe under the Innovation in Outcome Measurement (IOM) program
primary_use_case: Agronomic decision support and yield risk analysis
justification: Useful adaptation-oriented agronomic dataset, but geographically localized and commodity-specific.

output_variable_type: Nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium estimates, yield limiting factors, agronomic risk indicators

name: Dataset for the evaluation of forest and landscape restoration in Northern Ethiopia
short_description: This dataset was used to apply the Stochastic Impact Evaluation (SIE) framework in the simulation of returns on investment in biophysical and socio-economic interventions. The study was carried out in a degraded dry Afromontane forest in northern Ethiopia. We used an expert knowledge elicitation approach to acquire the data from decision-makers and community stakeholders. We also developed a probabilistic model of the decision’s costs and benefits while accounting for risks and uncertainties and projected outcomes over time. The data shared here captures all the parameters considered important in the impact pathway (i.e. the expected benefits, costs, and risks) of the decision. The data can be used to examine the impact of Forest Landscape Restoration (FLR) projects on livelihoods as well as landscapes.
primary_use_case: Restoration investment analysis and climate adaptation planning
justification: Strong restoration investment and adaptation planning relevance with transferable analytical framework.

name: ILCYM – Insect Life Cycle Modeling Platform for Pest and Natural Enemy Risk Modeling
asset_type: Modeling platform / decision-support software / pest risk modeling tool / climate-driven biological modeling asset
short_description: ILCYM, Insect Life Cycle Modeling, is a CIP-developed modeling platform for building temperature-driven insect phenology and population models, validating those models, and generating local, regional, or global pest risk maps. It supports the development of process-oriented, age-stage structured insect models based on experimental life-table data and climate inputs. In CIP workflows, ILCYM is used for pest and natural enemy modeling, biodiversity modeling, crop modeling, pest risk assessment, and climate-change impact analysis for crop protection.

primary_use_case: Development, validation, simulation, and spatial mapping of temperature-driven pest and natural enemy phenology models for crop protection, pest risk assessment, biodiversity modeling, and crop modeling.
justification: ILCYM is the highest-priority candidate in this submission because it is a CIP-developed climate-risk modelling platform that directly links temperature-driven insect biology with pest and natural enemy risk assessment. It supports development, validation, simulation and spatial mapping of insect phenology and population dynamics, making it highly relevant for crop protection, biodiversity modelling, crop modelling and climate-change impact analysis. Its strategic value lies in translating climate inputs into reusable pest-risk outputs that can inform surveillance, integrated pest management, phytosanitary risk assessment and adaptation planning across multiple crops and geographies. The tool has strong reuse potential because its framework can be adapted to different insect species where life-table data and climate inputs are available. Its main limitation is that each new species or geography requires biological data, validation and careful interpretation. Even with these limitations, ILCYM is one of CIP’s strongest examples of a climate-driven modelling asset with cross-program relevance.

name: Lateblight Advisor / SIMCAST Digital Advisory Platform for Potato Late Blight

short_description: Lateblight Advisor is a CIP digital advisory platform that runs the SIMCAST decision-support model for potato late blight caused by Phytophthora infestans. The platform combines observed and forecast weather data, crop dates, field location, country-specific cultivar lists, and cultivar resistance classes to generate weather-driven fungicide timing recommendations. It supports disease-risk modeling, spray scheduling, fungicide planning, and scalable advisory delivery for potato late blight management.

justification: Lateblight Advisor / SIMCAST is a top strategic Climate Data Hub candidate because it directly connects climate data to real-world advisory decisions for potato late blight management. The platform converts observed and forecast weather into cultivar-specific fungicide timing recommendations, helping reduce unnecessary applications while maintaining disease control. It is highly decision-relevant because it is designed for advisory delivery, farmer-facing use and extension support, and the scaling work documents training, user feedback, technical modernization and field demonstration trials. Its technical readiness is strong: the tool has moved to a Python-based implementation, integrates real-time weather data, exposes standardized model outputs through a SIMCAST MCP layer, and links to GeoSIMCAST for spatial risk mapping. Its main limitations are dependence on weather data quality, cultivar resistance metadata, local validation and user training. Overall, it is one of CIP’s clearest examples of climate-informed digital advisory infrastructure and should be prioritised for early Climate Data Hub visibility and federation.

name: SOLANUM / Spatial Solanum / SWEETPO Crop Growth Modeling Tools

SOLANUM estimates potato growth and yield under potential, water-limited and frost-limited conditions, making it directly relevant to crop modelling, breeding environment characterisation, adaptation analytics and climate-risk assessment. Spatial Solanum strengthens the asset by extending model outputs to spatial yield simulation, supporting agroclimatic zoning, crop suitability analysis and regional productivity assessment. SWEETPO broadens the family of tools to sweetpotato, increasing relevance for CIP’s root and tuber portfolio. The asset has high reuse potential because the modelling framework can be applied across sites, seasons and scenarios when appropriate weather, crop and management inputs are available. The key limitations are the need to confirm current maintenance status, model versioning, calibration scope and active operational use. Despite these caveats, it is highly aligned with Climate Data Hub priorities around reusable climate-crop analytics.

It should be included because it produces canopy-temperature and CWSI-related outputs for irrigation, adaptation and crop modelling workflows, but it is less central than assets with broader decision-support or spatial climate analytics roles.

output_variable_type: Canopy temperature, aligned RGB–thermal images, crop water stress indicators, CWSI, plot-level thermal phenotyping variables
user_groups: CIP Remote Sensing team; crop ecophysiologists; irrigation researchers; crop modelers; breeders; agronomists; geospatial analysts; Digital & Data Unit; field technicians collecting RGB/thermal imagery

name: Image Canopy – Digital Canopy Cover Estimation Software
asset_type: CGIAR-produced software tool / digital phenotyping tool / image-derived crop monitoring asset
short_description: Image Canopy is a CIP-developed software tool for estimating crop canopy cover from orthogonal RGB field images. It uses image segmentation to classify healthy green foliage and calculate the percentage of canopy cover, particularly in potato. The resulting canopy-cover indicators support digital phenotyping, crop growth monitoring, breeding evaluation, crop modeling, biodiversity modeling, and assessment of crop responses to water stress or environmental conditions.
commodity: Potato primarily; also relevant to sweetpotato and other crops where RGB canopy-cover protocols are validated
spatial_coverage: Flexible. Applicable wherever orthogonal RGB crop images are collected; documented primarily for potato and used in crop research contexts relevant to CIP.
spatial_resolution: Field / plot / plant image scale; depends on camera resolution, acquisition height, image protocol, and plot design.
year_last_updated: Version-dependent. The document lists Image Canopy Software v3.6; current maintenance status should be validated with the CIP Remote Sensing team.
url: Image Canopy Software v3.6: https://doi.org/10.21223/P3/50TASS ; Video: https://youtu.be/klc1_rzfRjg ; Practical field guide: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116857
file_format: Software-dependent. Inputs are RGB digital images; outputs may include canopy-cover percentage, segmented/masked images, and tabular summaries. Published software package available through DOI.
data_structure_type: Image-processing software; RGB field imagery; tabular image-derived canopy-cover outputs
primary_use_case: Digital phenotyping and crop growth monitoring through estimation of canopy-cover percentage from RGB field images, supporting crop modeling, breeding evaluation, drought-response studies, and biodiversity/crop adaptation analysis
justification: Supporting digital phenotyping asset that converts RGB images into canopy-cover indicators useful for crop growth monitoring, breeding evaluation and crop model validation. It is relevant but lower priority because its link to climate decision-making is indirect.
primary_climate_inputs: Not a climate dataset by itself. Can be linked with rainfall, temperature, irrigation treatment, drought stress, or water limitation in experiments or crop modeling workflows.
output_variable_type: Canopy cover percentage; segmented canopy images; plot-level vegetation cover indicators; crop growth and vigor indicators
user_groups: CIP Remote Sensing team; crop ecophysiologists; crop modelers; potato and sweetpotato breeders; agronomists; field technicians; geospatial analysts; Digital & Data Unit; researchers working on crop adaptation and stress physiology
cgiar_programs: CIP remote sensing and crop physiology workflows; crop modeling and digital phenotyping activities; potato and sweetpotato breeding support; biodiversity and crop adaptation analyses; Climate Action-related work where crop response to climate stress is relevant
partners: CIP Image Canopy software workflows; field phenotyping studies; potato canopy monitoring; sweetpotato drought tolerance and crop wild relative studies; water-stress and crop-growth studies; potential use by universities and breeding programs

name: Global MapSPAM 2020
short_description: Global spatial dataset of crop production, area, and yield at ~10 km resolution using data fusion methods
partners: AAA Atlas (foundational exposure dataset); Land and carbon lab from WRI, NASA harvest

name: Global hamonized cropland
short_description: Global cropland extent products that harmonized multiple existing cropland maps with multilevel cropland area statistics were developed using advanced statistical methods. The method ranks and integrates input cropland datasets based on their consistency with official statistics, then adaptively assigns cropland areas to pixels with stronger agreement across datasets.
partners: WRI, NASA harvest, U of Minnesota, Huazhong Agri Univ., Wageningen University

name: Time series of global harmonized cropland extent from 2011 to 2020
short_description: Time series of global harmonized cropland extent from 2011 to 2020 were developed using a machine-learning-based approach. The method combines multiple global land cover and cropland products derived from satellite observations, including Landsat and Sentinel, to identify the most reliable cropland information across countries and regions. By learning from the spatial and temporal consistency among input products, the harmonized dataset captures annual cropland dynamics while reducing uncertainty from individual products.
partners: U of Minnesota, Huazhong Agri Univ., Wageningen University

name: Crop specific suitablity layers
short_description: Crop-specific suitability layers are developed by reanalyzing and refining GAEZ data to estimate suitable area and potential yield for major crops. Rather than directly using the original GAEZ outputs, we further revise the suitability information to better capture spatial variation in crop potential based on crop-specific thermal, moisture, and soil requirements.
partners: Huazhong Agri Univ, China Agriculture Univ.

name: Global Irrigation map
short_description: The harmonized irrigation layers are generated by integrating existing historical irrigation maps available at different spatial scales.
partners: China Agriculture Univ.

name: Global Farming system data
short_description: Farming system data provides the share of irrigated and rainfed crop area by crop and country. The farming systems are mainly defined by water supply conditions and farmer input use, although global data on crop-specific farming system shares are generally limited. For large countries such as Brazil, China, India, Russia, and the United States, farming system shares are compiled at the ADM1 level. For other countries, national farming system are compiled at country level and then adjusted based on available supporting evidence.
partners: U of Minnesota

name: Global crop specific intensity data
short_description: Global crop intensity data provide information on the cropping intensity of individual crops, including single, double, and multiple cropping systems. The data are compiled by crop at the country level, and for selected large countries, at the ADM1 level, to better capture subnational variation in crop production intensity.
partners: U of Minnesota

name: Global subnational agricultural statistics
short_description: Global subnational agricultural statistics provide crop area, yield, and production data at subnational levels for recent years, with the latest update around 2020. Similar to FAOSTAT at the national level, this dataset compiles and harmonizes subnational agricultural statistics from various sources, including national statistical bureaus, agricultural yearbooks, and hard-copy reports. It improves the availability of detailed crop statistics for supporting spatial allocation, validation, and subnational agricultural analysis.
partners: WRI, NASA harvest, U of Minnesota, Huazhong Agri Univ., Wageningen University

name: High resolution national crop type maps
short_description: Country-specific crop type mapping is produced using a country-level allocation model with customized crop type selections for individual countries. The product provides higher-spatial-resolution crop type maps tailored to national crop systems and data availability, supporting more detailed agricultural monitoring, spatial analysis, and crop-specific planning
partners: country programs

name: Seasonal Crop type map from Foundation model
short_description: In-season crop type maps are generated using a foundation-model-based approach together with ground-truth data to identify crop types during the growing season. Using high-resolution 10-meter satellite imagery, the model maps crop types across entire counties, providing timely and detailed information for crop monitoring, in-season assessment, and agricultural decision-making.
partners: Uni of Eldoret

name: IMPACT Projections of Hunger With and Without Climate Change
short_description: Country-level IMPACT model projections of the population at risk of hunger (millions) to 2030 and 2050, comparing a climate-change scenario with a no-climate-change baseline, as published in Table 1C of the 2022 Global Food Policy Report annex; countries worldwide with aggregations to six developing regions (base period 2010). [AI-drafted from the Harvard Dataverse record and related publications - pending IFPRI review]

name: Ethiopia Nile Basin Climate Change Adaptation Dataset
short_description: Cross-sectional survey of 1,000 mixed crop-livestock farming households in the Ethiopian Nile Basin for the 2004/05 production year, conducted by IFPRI with the Ethiopian Development Research Institute across 20 woredas in five regional states, stratified by rainfall pattern, agro-ecological zone, food-production vulnerability and irrigation prevalence; covers demographics, assets, shocks, climate perceptions and adaptation responses. Basin-scoped, not nationally representative. [AI-drafted from the Harvard Dataverse record and related publications - pending IFPRI review]

name: Bangladesh Climate Change Adaptation Survey (BCCAS, Rounds I-II)
short_description: Panel survey of 800 farming households across Bangladesh's seven broad agro-ecological zones: Round I December 2010 - February 2011, Round II September-October 2012 re-interviewing ~97% of households (doi:10.7910/DVN/27883); covers demographics, social capital, land tenure, crop and livestock management, input use, extension, climatic shocks and adaptation options. Produced by IFPRI with Data Analysis and Technical Assistance (DATA) Ltd and ZEF, University of Bonn. [AI-drafted from the Harvard Dataverse record and related publications - pending IFPRI review]

name: IFPRI-CCAFS Gender and Climate Change Survey Data: Bangladesh
short_description: Gender-disaggregated household survey (April-May 2013) in 44 villages of Bagerhat and Satkhira districts, Bangladesh, under the CCAFS project 'Increasing Women's Resilience to Confront Climate Change', interviewing one male and one female decision-maker separately in ~416 households linked to ILRI's IMPACT Lite survey; covers climate perceptions, shocks, adaptation and coping, decision-making, asset ownership, information access, credit and group membership. [AI-drafted from the Harvard Dataverse record and related publications - pending IFPRI review]

name: South Africa Limpopo Basin Climate Change Adaptation Dataset
short_description: Household survey of 794 farm households across 19 districts, five Water Management Areas and four provinces in the South African Limpopo River Basin, covering the 2004/05 agricultural season (fieldwork August-November 2005); modules on demographics, farm and non-farm activities, assets, shocks, crop and livestock production, water use, climate perceptions and adaptation strategies. Collected by CEEPA (University of Pretoria) with IFPRI; twin of the Ethiopia Nile Basin dataset. [AI-drafted from the Harvard Dataverse record and related publications - pending IFPRI review]

name: Plant species of Drabo Gbo Forest Benin
short_description: Comprehensive inventory of plant species recorded in the Drabo Gbo Forest (a rehabilitated secondary rainforest in southern Benin), documenting species composition, origin, and survival status following long-term ecological restoration efforts.
justification: This asset represents a detailed floristic dataset of plant species in a restored forest ecosystem in Benin, which may hold value for biodiversity monitoring and ecological restoration research. Its strategic value likely lies in documenting long-term species composition and survival in a rehabilitated tropical forest, supporting studies on restoration outcomes and species diversity. The dataset may be relevant for contemporary efforts in ecosystem restoration, conservation planning, and baseline biodiversity assessments. Reuse potential could include comparative ecological studies or informing restoration practices in similar environments.
output_variable_type: Species occurrence, taxonomic identity, origin classification, and survival status
user_groups: Researchers, Governments, Donors, Private sector, Local communities

name: Predicted aflatoxin risk in maize
short_description: Machine learning– and satellite data–driven system that predicts and maps pre-harvest aflatoxin contamination risk in maize across East and Southern Africa, classifying risk levels and supporting targeted interventions via an interactive dashboard.
justification: This asset provides strategic value as a data-driven tool for anticipating aflatoxin contamination risk in maize, a major food safety and public health concern in East and Southern Africa. By translating climate and environmental signals into spatial risk classifications, it supports timely, targeted interventions across the value chain. Its contemporary relevance is high given increasing climate variability, which influences fungal growth and toxin production. The model’s outputs have reuse potential for decision-support in extension services, policy planning, and food safety monitoring, and could be adapted to similar agro-ecological contexts or other susceptible crops. Model performance depends on the availability and quality of historical contamination data. Some regions may be underrepresented in training data. Operational aspects (e.g., real-time deployment, update frequency) are not fully detailed.
primary_climate_inputs: Temperature; rainfall; humidity; Soil moisture; Vapour deficit, drought indicators
user_groups: Researchers, Governments, Private sector, Grain handlers
partners: KARLO, East Africa Grain Council

name: Soybean rust (SBR) risk hotspot
short_description: Develop a predictive model for soybean rust (SBR) risk using environmental, climate, and satellite-derived data to support early warning and disease management.
justification: Demonstrates the feasibility of AI-driven plant disease forecasting, with strong potential for scaling across crops and regions. Provides predictive disease risk maps, as a framework for early warning systems and decision-support tools to inform on when to spray with fungicides. Key limitations relate to an imbalance of disease risk data due to uneven surveillance across regions. Update frequency and operational maintenance are not clearly documented, while performance metrics not visible in the interface.
primary_climate_inputs: rainfall, temperature, humidity, Wind speed, dewpoint, temperature, Solar radiation,  soil moisture, PDSI, Evapotranspiration and interception
partners: Good Nature Agro (GNA), Solidaridad Malawi

name: SoySA10
short_description: National 10-m soybean maps for South Africa from 2018 to 2025
hub_role: Supporting agricultural land-use analysis, crop monitoring, and assessment of soybean expansion or change over time using consistent remote sensing methods under changing climate.
justification: SoySA10 provides national-scale 10 m soybean distribution maps for South Africa from 2018–2025, offering a recent and spatially detailed dataset for monitoring soybean cultivation dynamics. Its strategic value lies in supporting agricultural land-use analysis, crop monitoring, and assessment of soybean expansion or change over time using consistent remote sensing methods. The dataset is contemporarily relevant because soybean production is closely linked to food systems, trade, and climate-sensitive agricultural planning in southern Africa. Important limitations should be considered: validation metrics indicate stronger performance for non-soybean classes than soybean identification, and classification uncertainty may vary across regions and seasons. The dataset is specific to South Africa and may not generalize directly to other geographic contexts without methodological adaptation.
output_variable_type: Soybean distribution, soybean field type and soybean cultivation change map
partners: University of Twente, Agricultural Research Council-Natural Resource & Engineering

name: HFCP Nigeria
short_description: High frequency crowdsourced commodity prices in Nigeria
commodity: Maize, garri, yam, rice, sorghum, white beans, brown beans, soybeans
hub_role: Use case for promoting transparency, accountability, and evidence-based decision-making in food pricing
justification: The high frequency commodity prices are valuable resource for policy and planning. For instance, government agencies can use the data to inform food subsidy programs, inflation targeting, and emergency response planning. Citizens can access localized price information to make informed purchasing decisions. By democratizing access to food price data, the Initiative fosters transparency, accountability, and evidence-based decision-making in Nigeria’s food system. Prices were collected across markets by volunteers and trained enumerators. Although the submissions have been thoroughly validated, the dataset do not replace official market prices and statistics from the focal country.
output_variable_type: Food prices, market dynamics, food security, and economic resilience
partners: WorldBank, JRC, Nigeria, National Bureau of Statistics

name: Citizen-H2D3
short_description: Citizen-driven Individual/ Household Dietary Diversity Dynamics
climate_domain: Diet quality, nutrition monitoring, population health
hub_role: To provide high-frequency monitoring of the Minimum Dietary Diversity for Women (MDD-W) to reveal seasonal diet fluctuations tied to Rwanda's agricultural calendar.
justification: This dataset is strategically valuable for supporting research and policy analysis related to diet quality, nutrition monitoring, and population health trends. High-frequency mapping approaches may enable more timely insights than conventional periodic surveys, which is particularly relevant in contexts where food systems, prices, and consumption patterns are changing rapidly. The contemporary relevance is strengthened by growing interest in data-driven approaches to public health, food security, and citizen-generated data initiatives. However data is limited only for Rwanda.
output_variable_type: Diet diversity, Food basket, Diet quality, Food group, age, sex, economic quantile
partners: VIAMO

name: PlantVillage Nuru
short_description: PlantVillage Nuru in-field Pest and Disease Diagnosis
hub_role: Pest and disease advisory based on climate change and variability
primary_use_case: Research, Policy, Investment targeting, Disease control
justification: Nuru: In-field Pest and Disease Diagnosis has strategic value as a digital agricultural support asset designed to assist with rapid field-level identification of crop pests and diseases. Its relevance remains high in the context of increasing interest in precision agriculture, climate-related crop stress monitoring, and mobile decision-support tools for smallholder farming systems. The asset may support improved response times, extension services, and data-informed crop management where validated diagnostic workflows are available.

name: Sims

name: Pan-African Soybean Variety Trials (PAT)
short_description: PAT is a large-scale trials dataset from multi-environment trials (METs) conducted in Africa over ten years. It includes 292 trials carried out in 138 locations across 21 countries, 26,280 plots, evaluating 366 soybean varieties. The dataset contains data from morphologic, agronomic and quality traits, as well as detailed environmental information, such as soil features, weather variables, and management practices.
justification: Dataset is valuable for advancing research on genotype × environment × management interactions and adaptation of soybean germplasm, supporting genetic evaluation, environmental modeling, and informed decision-making for soybean expansion in Africa

name: Nutrient omission, planting window, crop arrangement, and management data in Chinyanja Triangle
short_description: On-station and on-farm datasets on nutrient omission trials, varieties, planting window, and crop management of maize and soybean in Zambia, Malawi, and Mozambique from 2022/2023 and 2023/2024 seasons. These datasets contain data on crop physiology, growth, yields and soil characteristics of the on-station sites.
user_groups: Researchers, Governments, Donors, Private sector, Local communities
partners: DARs, SLU, IIAM, ZARI

name: System dynamics model for exploring synergies, trade-offs, and resilience
short_description: This dashboard provides a system dynamics model (SDM) platform to promote a holistic understanding of cascading impacts of interlinked feedback loops in cereal-legume-livestock mixed farming systems. The developed SDM enables the assessment of impacts of the adoption of cowpea living mulch (CPLM) intercropping technology on four ustainable agriculture intensification domains, i.e. production, environment, economic, and human condition
justification: This asset has strategic value because it applies a system dynamics modelling approach to examine interactions within cereal–legume–livestock mixed farming systems in northern Ghana. By focusing on synergies, trade-offs, and resilience, the work may support more integrated agricultural planning and improve understanding of how different farm components interact under changing environmental and socio-economic conditions. Such approaches are increasingly important for designing sustainable and climate-resilient farming systems in sub-Saharan Africa. The asset remains contemporarily relevant given ongoing concerns about food security, climate variability, soil fertility management, and sustainable intensification. Its systems-based perspective may be useful for researchers, development practitioners, policymakers, and agricultural extension initiatives working on mixed farming or resilience-oriented interventions. There is also potential for reuse in comparative modelling studies, training on systems thinking approaches, and adaptation to similar agroecological contexts. However, limitations are likely related to context specificity, assumptions embedded within the model, and possible constraints in data availability or validation. The available information is insufficient to assess model performance, scalability, or reproducibility in detail.
output_variable_type: Yield, Soil Moisture, Soil nutrients, Labour, Income, Livestock production
user_groups: Researchers, Governments, Donors, Private sector, Local communities

name: HarvestStat Africa
short_description: HarvestStat Africa is a subset or regional application of the HarvestStat database providing harmonized agricultural production, area, and yield statistics across African countries, compiled from national and international sources to support research, policy analysis, and food security monitoring. The dataset includes 574,204 records, primarily spanning from 1980 to 2022, detailing quantity produced, harvested areas, and yields for 33 countries and 94 crop types.
justification: This new dataset enhances our understanding of how climate variability and change influence agricultural production, supports subnational food system analysis, and aids in operational yield forecasting. As an open-source it establishes a precedent for sharing subnational crop statistics to inform decision-making and modeling efforts. However, subnational crop statistics in Sub-Saharan Africa may exhibit inherent uncertainty due to technical errors, such as sampling, processing, and coverage errors in agricultural census statistics. Measuring harvested area accurately is challenging without advanced techniques, which are often not available in various regions, especially in past decades. The availability of crop statistics in Sub-Saharan Africa is often discontinuous in both space and time.
output_variable_type: Yield, Production, Harvest Area, Planting date, Name of plots
user_groups: Researchers, Governments, Donors, Private sector, Local communities

name: Bias-corrected climate dataset for East Africa
short_description: Bias-corrected climate data for East Africa
data_structure_type: Raster, Time series, Model output, Processed input dataset
justification: This asset is strategically important because it translates climate observations and seasonal forecast information into practical agriculture indicators: onset, cessation and length of growing season. These outputs are directly relevant to smallholder advisories, planting-window decisions, dry-spell preparedness and seasonal risk communication. The asset is contemporary because it relies on CHIRPS and seasonal forecast products such as SEAS5, combined with reproducible detection and bias-correction workflows. Reuse potential is high because the same structure can be adapted to different countries, seasons, crops and administrative reporting units. The main limitations are that local onset/cessation rules, thresholds, forecast calibration choices and uncertainty products must be validated with domain experts and national stakeholders before formal submission.
user_groups: Government, NGOs, Donors, Internal research teams
partners: EMI, SMHI, ILRI

name: Temperature-Related Stress Maps
short_description: The Rice Temperature Stress Assessment product identifies areas where rice crops may be vulnerable to temperature-related stresses that can adversely affect plant growth, development, and yield. The assessment evaluates the frequency and occurrence of temperatures exceeding critical thresholds for rice production during a cropping season, based on at least 20 years of historical climate data and future climate projections. Stress incidence is determined by estimating the number of days when temperatures fall outside the crop's optimal range. The product covers three major types of temperature stress: daytime heat stress, nighttime heat stress, and cold stress, providing valuable information for climate risk assessment, agricultural planning, and the development of adaptation strategies for rice production.

name: Country-level DSR Suitability Assessment Maps
short_description: Direct-seeded Rice (DSR) offers significant advantages over traditional rice farming practice of transplanting. Based on a method, developed by colleagues in India, Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) was done to come up with weights of factors that could drive suitability of DSR farming.  The identified factors used include temperature, precipitation, evapotranspiration, slope, soil depth, soil texture, and soil moisture.

name: Rice-GEM
short_description: RiceGEM (Rice Computation Engine for Greenhouse Gas Emissions) is a digital tool that automates the calculation of methane (CH₄) and nitrous oxide (N₂O) emissions from flooded paddy fields, serving as a critical component for climate-smart agricultural frameworks in Southeast Asia.

name: NDVI-Anomaly based Drought Maps
short_description: Apart from vegetation, NDVI can also indicate water or the lack of it. We tried to determine drought by finding areas that show lower than normal (non-negative) NDVI from NASA's MODIS vegetation product. The analysis comes up with a drought incidence map, estimated average length of dry period, and estimated frequency of dry events over a target region.
spatial_coverage: PHL, MRD, Ganges Delta of Bangladesh

name: AWD Suitability Maps
short_description: Alternate wetting and drying (AWD) is one of the most effective methane emission reduction strategies available for rice farming. However, this technique requires a certain level of control over water that comes in and stays in a rice field. By analyzing climatic water balance and soil water retention, we tried to ascertain where AWD is feasible to implement around the globe.

name: NDFI-based Flood Risk Assessment
short_description: Using MODIS surface reflectance layers, a time-series of Normalized Difference Flood Index (NDFI) was calculated and analyzed to detect inundation annually and eventually calculate incidence over a period of 20 years. Average duration and number of flooding events are calculated as well.
spatial_coverage: PHL, MRD, Ganges Delta of Bangladesh

name: IRRI's LTCCE
short_description: The LTCCE at IRRI stands for Long-Term Continuous Cropping Experiment. It's a long-term research setup at the International Rice Research Institute (IRRI) designed to study the effects of continuous cropping (growing rice year after year on the same land) on:soil fertility, rice yields, nutrient management, and sustainability. The LTCCE at IRRI is like a “time machine” experiment that studies how growing rice continuously on the same land affects yields and soil health, helping design long-lasting, sustainable rice farming systems.

name: RCMAS
short_description: Rice Crop Manager Advisory Service (RCMAS) is a digital decision-support tool developed by the Philippine Department of Agriculture, Philippine Rice Research Institute, and International Rice Research Institute to help rice farmers make better crop management decisions.

name: Rice Mitigation Hotspots and Mitigation Pathway Maps (currently being developed)
short_description: Using a combination of earth observation and rice modelling data, this product identifies current rice emissions hotspots, their drivers, and priority mitigation pathways across various intervention types.
name_submitted: Rice Mitigation Hotspots and Mitigation Pathway Maps (currently being develop)

name: Africa Multi-hazard risk maps
short_description: Multi-Hazard Map integrates data on various natural disasters to assess risk exposure and support climate adaptation. This map visualizes hazard (floods, droughts, cyclones, landslides, epidemics, volcanic activity, earthquakes, extreme temperatures, and wildfires) occurrences across Africa from 1950 to 2020, categorizing disasters by type, frequency, and impact at district level. It provides spatially disaggregated data to enhance decision-making in disaster risk management.
commodity: Crop-specific coverage including maize, wheat, rice, soybean, sorghum, millet, barley, oil palm, rapeseed, sunflower, sugarcane, sugar beet, and potato (coverage varies by region)
partners: AAA Atlas Impacts Notebook

name: Africa Flood Hazard
short_description: The Africa flood hazard dataset utilizes Earth Observation (EO) data to deliver continent-wide flood monitoring at a 500 m spatial resolution. It is developed using moderate-resolution NASA MODIS data spanning 2001 to 2025 and is available at continental, national, and sub-national levels across Africa.
partners: AAA Atlas (foundational exposure dataset)

name: Surface Water Extent
short_description: The surface water extent layer, developed using Sentinel-1 SAR data, provides high-resolution mapping at 10 m across the entire South Asia region. It captures both fine- and large-scale water bodies, offering detailed insights into surface water distribution for the years 2020 and 2024.

name: Africa Drought Hazard
short_description: The Africa-wide drought hazard map is developed at the Admin-2 level using the Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) (SPI-3, 6, 9 and 12) to capture short to long-term drought conditions. The SPI dataset is derived from Global Precipitation Measurement (GPM) rainfall data spanning the period from 2001 to 2025, enabling consistent and spatially detailed drought monitoring across the continent.

name: Irrigated Area Map
short_description: The data set is developed based on phenological variations in agricultural areas to distinguish between irrigated and rainfed systems. A time-series dataset derived from the MOD13Q1 of MODIS at 250 m spatial resolution is utilized to capture crop growth stages. Advanced signal processing techniques, including Fourier and Wavelet transformations, are applied to analyze Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) patterns and effectively map agricultural phenology.

name: River Discharge
short_description: This dataset comprises station-based daily river discharge measurements from 25 locations, including 15 stations in Sri Lanka and 10 in Zambia. The temporal coverage varies by station, ranging from approximately 10 to 25 years, depending on the availability and continuity of historical records.

name: Population Impact on Flood
short_description: Flood-exposed population data are derived by integrating flood extent layers (2001–2025) with WorldPop data. This dataset identifies population hotspots vulnerable to flooding and is structured as a 24-layer stack, enabling detailed spatial analysis of exposure patterns over time.

name: South Asia Climate Risk Map
short_description: An integrated, multi-hazard climate risk dataset combining flood, drought, and heat stress indicators with population, agriculture, and infrastructure exposure layers. The map identifies regional risk hotspots across South Asia, supporting spatial prioritization, resilience planning, and targeted investment decisions.

name: Future fish biomass projections (DBEM / FishMIP)
short_description: The Dynamic Bioclimate Envelope Model (DBEM) database provides global, annual projections of marine fish biomass and distribution on a 0.5° × 0.5° (~50 km) grid, covering a historical baseline and future periods to 2100. It is produced within the Fish‑MIP / ISIMIP3b framework, driven by CMIP6 climate models under SSP1‑2.6 and SSP5‑8.5, with experiments including climate‑only and climate‑plus‑fishing scenarios.
justification: Offers species-level climate projections (compared to the FishMIP ensemble which is total biomass only). Because it is a single model it includes fewer uncertainty estimates.
primary_climate_inputs: Sea temperature, primary productivity, oxygen (from CMIP-driven ocean models)
partners: Fish‑MIP; ISIMIP; UBC; FAO impact studies

name: FishBase
short_description: Global biodiversity information system providing species‑level data on the taxonomy, biology, life history, ecology, distribution, and uses of all known finfish species, based on curated literature and expert contributions.
justification: Foundational Climate Action enabler. FishBase underpins climate impact models, exposure assessments, and adaptation analyses by providing species‑level biological and ecological traits. Its interoperability and long‑term maintenance make it a cornerstone dataset for climate‑fisheries integration.
partners:
FAO; Sea Around Us; IPBES; 
national research institutes

name: FishScores
short_description: Web‑based calculator that provides a standardized, rapid environmental impact assessment for aquaculture production, estimating greenhouse gas emissions, nitrogen and phosphorus loading, and water and land use based on farm and feed inputs.
justification: Useful for specific mitigation and industry decision contexts, but limited relevance for national or system‑level Climate Action analytics within a Hub framework.
output_variable_type: Environmental footprint indicators (GHG, N, P, land, water)
user_groups: Internal, Government, Donor, Private Sector, NGOs
partners: Sustainable Fisheries Partnership; aquaculture improvement projects

name: Bio-Oracle
short_description: Global database of marine environmental data layers providing physical, chemical, and biological variables for surface and benthic oceans at 0.05° (~5 km) resolution, supporting species distribution modelling, macroecology, and climate‑change impact assessments under present and CMIP6‑SSP future scenarios.
justification: Bio‑ORACLE provides the biophysical climate drivers required to assess exposure and vulnerability of marine systems. Its direct use in climate modelling and adaptation analytics makes it a high‑priority asset for supporting Climate Action across scales.
partners:
Species distribution modelling community
 climate‑impact studies
contact_email_source: Google Scholar (verified cgiar.org); WorldFish staff profile; ERL 2024 corresponding author

name: Sea Around Us
short_description: Global fisheries database providing reconstructed catches, fishing effort, stock status indicators, subsidies, and spatially explicit fisheries statistics for all marine regions, supporting fisheries assessment, policy analysis, and climate‑impact studies.
justification: supports Climate Action by revealing fishing pressure, trends, and inequalities not visible in official data. Its comparative value makes it important for policy debate and strategic analysis, though it requires careful framing.
partners: FAO; WorldFish; national and regional partners

name: FishStatJ
short_description: Desktop software application providing access to global fisheries and aquaculture statistical time series, including capture production, aquaculture production, trade, and food balance data, with tools for data extraction, aggregation, charting, and reporting.
justification: FishStatJ provides statistical baseline required for climate risk assessment, adaptation planning, mitigation tracking, and reporting (e.g. SDGs, NDCs). Its authority, global coverage, and sustainability make it indispensable as a core  dataset supporting real‑world decisions.
asset_organization: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO)
partners: FAO; national fisheries authorities

name: Illumating Hidden Harvests
short_description: Global initiative generating and disseminating evidence on the contributions, impacts, and governance of small‑scale fisheries, addressing data gaps in catch, employment, nutrition, and livelihoods to inform policy, empower fishing communities, and support sustainable and equitable fisheries.
justification: High policy impact for equity and small‑scale fisheries, but designed as a one‑off assessment rather than a continuously updated data asset. Can support climate vulnerability assessments but needs country-level agreements for national statistics. Regional ones are easier to obtain.
partners: FAO, Duke University, WorldFish

name: Aquatic Food Composition Database
short_description: Global, harmonized database synthesizing nutrient composition data for aquatic food species, compiled from national food composition tables, FAO/INFOODS datasets, and peer‑reviewed literature to support nutrition, food‑systems, and policy analyses.
justification: Climate–nutrition integration asset. AFCD supports climate decisions by linking aquatic foods to nutrition outcomes, helping design climate‑smart, nutrition‑sensitive food systems. Strong reuse potential justifies Hub integration.
user_groups: Internal, Government, Donor, Private Sector, NGOs
partners:
FAO INFOODS, Blue Foods Assessment,
 Harvard University
